items.q: What does the dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia state?
items.correct: Positive symptoms are linked to excess dopamine activity in mesolimbic pathways.
items.misconception: Schizophrenia is caused by too little dopamine.
items.random_wrong: Schizophrenia is caused by too much serotonin.
items.why: Students reverse the direction. The original hypothesis was HYPERdopaminergia. The revised version notes hypoactivity in prefrontal areas for negative symptoms, but excess in mesolimbic for positive.

items.q: How do SSRIs help with OCD?
items.correct: They prevent reabsorption of serotonin, increasing its availability in the synapse.
items.misconception: They directly produce more serotonin in the brain.
items.why: Students think SSRIs create serotonin. They actually inhibit REUPTAKE - serotonin already released stays in the synapse longer, increasing post-synaptic stimulation.

items.correct: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to INCREASE a behaviour.
items.misconception: Punishing a behaviour to decrease it.
items.random_wrong: Giving a reward to decrease behaviour.
items.why: Students confuse negative reinforcement with punishment. Both involve something unpleasant, but reinforcement (positive or negative) always INCREASES behaviour; punishment decreases it.

items.q: Lifetime risk of schizophrenia in the general population is approximately what?
items.why: It's 1%. “5%” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which receptors do typical antipsychotics primarily block?
items.why: Correct answer: Dopamine D2. “Serotonin 5-HT” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many candidate SNPs did Ripke 2014 identify for schizophrenia?
items.why: The real figure is 108. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
